region_of_origin: India (Southern India and Mughal culinary tradition)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mutton Kurma; mapped to the closest parent 'mutton dish' class (FOODON_03301116) as the nearest defensible ancestor. A dedicated term for South Indian / Mughlai mutton kurma does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dish, cooked, with sauce — mutton/lamb based
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Mutton Kurma entry; A0CKP (cooked meat dish with sauce) is the closest defensible parent category for a slow-simmered mutton curry with coconut-cashew gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps specifically to Mutton Kurma; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: roasted gram (chana dal) or poppy seeds

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Mutton Kurma is approximately 250 g (one bowl), which provides roughly 486 kcal, 32 g protein, 34 g fat, and 13 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Mutton Kurma are derived from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for cooked mutton, coconut, and cashew nut, combined with standard recipe proportions for South Indian Kurma. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g edible portion. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (mutton/lamb cooked) for plausibility verification.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — analogous estimate for low-GI meat-and-coconut curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Kurma in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dominant ingredients: mutton (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate), coconut cream (GI ~35–40, low), and cashew nuts (GI ~22, low). The dish contains minimal starch and no added sugar. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (35 × 5.14 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 4.5 g, confirming low GL. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium content (271 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from coconut and red meat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced salt during preparation, and moderate coconut cream to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.14 g/100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Kurma is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200 g) due to its low carbohydrate load and low GI; pair with high-fibre sides such as brown rice or whole-wheat roti rather than refined white rice or parotta to maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (5.71 g/100 g) from coconut cream and red meat, and cholesterol (51.43 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Kurma to occasional consumption (once weekly), choose lean mutton cuts, substitute coconut cream with reduced-fat coconut milk, and avoid adding ghee to keep saturated fat within the recommended daily limit of less than 20 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cardamom, cinnamon) balanced against pro-inflammatory potential of red meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice base may offer modest benefit; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to red meat should consider lean cuts or reduced mutton portions and monitor personal inflammatory response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Kurma is a rich, aromatic slow-cooked meat dish originating from South Indian and Mughlai culinary traditions. It is prepared by simmering tender bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in a freshly ground paste of coconut, cashew nuts, and roasted gram or poppy seeds, enriched with whole spices including cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon, and aromatics such as ginger, garlic, and green chillies. The resulting gravy is light-coloured, mildly spiced, and creamy in texture — distinctly different from red-chilli-forward curries. The dish is celebrated for its subtle sweetness from coconut and the warm fragrance of whole spices. Per 100 g, Mutton Kurma provides approximately 194 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 13.7 g fat (5.7 g saturated), and 5.1 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Its estimated glycemic index of 35 (low) makes it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at 271 mg/100 g, warranting portion awareness for those with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is traditionally served at festive occasions, Sunday lunches, and dinner gatherings, paired with layered parotta, appam, or aromatic pulao. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions, but should be consumed occasionally by those with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content from coconut cream and red meat.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for cooked mutton, coconut, and cashew nut using standard South Indian Kurma recipe proportions. No single approved-source entry exists for the exact finished dish 'Mutton Kurma'; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database ingredient-level data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite Mutton Kurma dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a finished-dish entry; only total fat and saturated fat are reported with confidence. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the finished dish is not reported in the available approved sources. 2. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag under cultural.dietary_tags, which is factually incorrect for a mutton-based dish; this tag has been removed. 3. Consolidated four duplicate '1 serving (250g)' portion entries into a single 250 g default serving. The 350 g 'grabfood' portion has been noted but not used as the default. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Kurma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.14 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.5 g (low). 5. The input source 'Indian Journal of Medical Research — Coconut in Health and Disease' is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central are the approved sources used. 6. Telugu alias 'మటన్ కుర్మా' is preserved from the input as a regional alias; Telugu is not a mandatory language under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for this cuisine category but is included for completeness. 7. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
